clues: This medication is often referred to as 'yellows' on the street due to the notorious yellow color of some tablets although different strengths are white and blue
Binding to specific sites on GABA receptors to increase GABA affinity, this particular medication has a extended half life of 20-50 hours
America's top-selling prescription drug from 1969 to 1982, this medication was famously referenced in the Rollings Stone song 'Mother's Little Helper' highlighting its widespread use
This disease is historically recognized in some African societies as 'ogbanjes' or 'children who come and go' due to high infant mortality rates
In some reports, the average lifespan of individuals affected is about 20 years less than those without. Bone marrow transplant is currently the only known cure
The mutation causing this disease is estimated to have arisen over 50,000 years ago, providing a survival advantage against malaria
With a narrow therapeutic window, this medication was used by Charles Cullen while he was working as a nurse to murder people in the 1990s

Although some doubt this claim, others believe that Vincent van Gogh experienced xanthopsia and other visual changes related to toxicity from this medication contributing to paintings such as The Starry Night and Sunflowers
Derived from the foxglove plant, this medication was used as a herbal remedy in the 1700s for dropsy
Used for over 3,000 years in other countries, this is used primarily for pain management in the United States, although some also use it for chemo-induced nausea and vomiting
Although skeptics sometimes claim the effect is placebo, studies have shown this therapy also works on animals
Originating in China, legend suggests this practice was discovered with soldiers wounded by arrows in battle found their unrelated chronic ailments were cured
Although frequently considered a disease of the past, cases of this are rising in developed countries due to increased cost of living, particularly in pediatric populations
Early symptoms were mistaken for extreme laziness in the past, but in advanced stages this disease can cause skin to turn black and year-old wounds to reopen
Between 1500 and 1800, this killed more sailors than storms, shipwrecks, and combat combined due to their diet of non-perishable, dried foods
